description: Canonical semantic keys for the Chronos knowledge graph. Each key is a concept-handle used by the AI inference layer to propose connections and estimate relationships.

keys.description: The philosophical and political ideal of ordered, harmonious relationships between individuals and social groups, especially prominent in Confucian and related traditions.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from 'political stability' (which is a structural outcome) — this refers to the normative ideal itself.

keys.description: Respect and care for parents, elders, and ancestors as a moral and social obligation; central to Confucian ethics.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Related to ancestor veneration but more focused on living family relations.

keys.description: The proper performance of ritual actions as constitutive of social order and moral cultivation; the concept of li in Confucian thought.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Not purely religious — in Confucian usage, ritual is as much about social structure as sacred practice.

keys.description: The theory and practice of organizing and directing political communities — who rules, by what authority, and toward what ends.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Broad parent key; prefer more specific child keys when applicable.

keys.description: Political legitimacy derived from or identified with divine authority; the ruler as mediator between human and divine orders.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from theocracy (clerical rule) — the king is himself divine or divinely appointed.

keys.description: Organized administrative structures for governing complex societies; systems for selecting and deploying administrators.
The codification, interpretation, and application of legal norms — from ancient law codes to developed jurisprudence.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Use for both legal philosophy and practical legal systems.

keys.description: Systematic inquiry into moral norms, virtues, obligations, and the nature of the good life.
The idea that the individual's primary moral task is continuous development of character, wisdom, or spiritual attainment.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Common to Confucianism, Stoicism, Buddhism, and Neoplatonism, but with different aims.

keys.description: Systematic rational inquiry into the fundamental nature of reality, knowledge, value, mind, and existence.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Top-level key; use child keys for specific branches.

keys.description: Inquiry into the fundamental nature of reality: being, substance, causation, time, space, and existence itself.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinguish from cosmology as astronomy. This is philosophical cosmology.

keys.description: The study of the nature, sources, scope, and limits of human knowledge.
The pre-disciplinary inquiry into the natural world — the ancestor of both physics and biology — primarily associated with Greek antiquity and the Islamic transmission.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from modern natural science; emphasizes philosophical rather than experimental method.

keys.description: Philosophical inquiry into the nature of political authority, justice, the ideal state, and political obligation.
The systematic study of valid inference, argument, and formal reasoning.
The practice of severe self-discipline and abstention from worldly pleasures as a path to spiritual development or liberation.
Organized systems of belief, practice, and community oriented around the sacred, the divine, or ultimate reality.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Top-level key; use child keys for specific traditions or concepts.

keys.description: Direct experiential knowledge of or union with ultimate reality, transcending ordinary rational categories.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Appears in Islamic Sufism, Jewish Kabbalah, Christian mysticism, Hindu advaita — do not conflate unless connections are specific.

keys.description: Religious systems recognizing multiple gods, typically with differentiated divine functions and narratives.
Religious traditions affirming the existence and worship of a single deity.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from henotheism (one god among many elevated) and monolatry (worship of one among many).

keys.description: The transmission of knowledge, narrative, and religious material through speech and memory rather than writing.
The processes by which intellectual traditions, texts, and techniques move across time, languages, and cultures.
The work of translating texts from one language to another as a vehicle for cross-cultural intellectual transmission.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Specifically about translation as a historical act; distinct from general scholarship.

keys.description: The support of artists, scholars, and intellectuals by rulers and their courts as a vehicle for cultural production.
The production, theorization, and appreciation of art; aesthetic theory and artistic traditions.
The production and circulation of written literary works — poetry, drama, historiography, philosophical dialogue.
Systematic empirical inquiry into the natural world, broadly construed to include both modern and pre-modern forms.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Top-level key; use child keys for specific disciplines.

keys.description: The study of abstract structure, quantity, and space; foundational to scientific and philosophical inquiry.
The observation and mathematical modeling of celestial bodies; historically entangled with astrology and calendrical reckoning.
keys.ambiguity_notes: In pre-modern contexts, astronomy and astrology were not fully separated.

keys.description: The theory and practice of healing the human body; includes both empirical medicine and humoral or other theoretical frameworks.
Practical techniques and tools that shape material life, production, and power.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from 'science' as organized theoretical inquiry; technology is practical application.

keys.description: Technologies for recording language in durable form; their invention and diffusion are transformative historical events.
Techniques and tools for ocean navigation, shipbuilding, and maritime commerce.
Techniques of crop cultivation, irrigation, animal husbandry, and food production.
Weapons, fortifications, tactics, and organizational forms that enable organized violence.
Organized armed conflict between groups; its causes, conduct, and consequences.
The expansion of political authority over diverse peoples and territories through conquest or coercion.
The exchange of goods across local, regional, and interregional networks.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Top-level key; use child keys for specific trade types or routes.

keys.description: Trade conducted by sea, including the great oceanic networks of the ancient and medieval world.
Trade conducted by land routes — caravans, steppe networks, and mountain passes.
The network of overland and maritime trade routes connecting China, Central Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.
keys.ambiguity_notes: A metaphorical umbrella term for a complex of routes, not a single road.

keys.description: The emergence and growth of cities as centers of political, economic, and cultural life.
The blending of distinct religious, philosophical, or cultural traditions into new syntheses.
The condition of living outside one's homeland, whether by forced expulsion or voluntary departure; often a vehicle for cultural transmission.
The intellectual tradition of the medieval European universities, characterized by systematic commentary, dialectical method, and synthesis of Greek philosophy with Christian theology.
The philosophical tradition derived from Plato through Plotinus, emphasizing the emanation of reality from a single transcendent source.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from Plato's own work; shapes Islamic, Jewish, and Christian medieval thought.

keys.description: The Hellenistic and Roman philosophical school emphasizing the logos, virtue as the sole good, and equanimity in the face of circumstance.
The philosophical stance of suspending judgment about knowledge claims, questioning the possibility of certainty.
The branch of philosophy concerned with the good life, virtue, duty, and moral obligation.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Overlaps heavily with 'ethics' as a parent; use for specific ethical schools or debates.

keys.description: In Indian religious and philosophical traditions, the cosmic order, moral duty, and proper way of living.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Meaning differs between Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain usage — use context to disambiguate.

keys.description: The goal of release from suffering, rebirth, or the cycle of conditioned existence in Indian and related traditions.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Moksha (Hindu), nirvana (Buddhist), mukti (Jain) are related but distinct concepts.

keys.description: The philosophical doctrine that all phenomena are transient and in constant flux; central to Buddhism and present in pre-Socratic thought.
The political management of economic resources, production, and distribution; the relationship between economic and political power.
Rule by the citizenry through direct participation or representation; associated particularly with ancient Athens and later republican traditions.
Rule by an individual without constitutional constraint; analyzed by Greek philosophers as the corruption of monarchy.
Political organization oriented around the public good, civic participation, and non-domination; associated with Rome and later the Atlantic revolutions.
The idea that all humans share a common moral community transcending national or ethnic boundaries.
The culture, thought, and civilization of ancient Greece and its subsequent diffusion through Alexander's conquests and the Hellenistic kingdoms.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Distinct from 'Greek religion' (polytheism) — refers broadly to Greek cultural influence.

keys.description: The cultural and intellectual heritage of ancient Greece and Rome, roughly 800 BCE – 500 CE.
The spread of Roman language, law, institutions, and culture across the Mediterranean world.
The philosophical and ethical tradition founded on the teachings of Confucius, emphasizing social harmony, ritual propriety, and benevolent governance.
The Chinese philosophical and religious tradition associated with the Dao (Way) — emphasizing naturalness, wu wei (non-action), and harmony with the cosmos.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Philosophical Daoism (Laozi, Zhuangzi) is distinct from later religious Daoism, though related.

keys.description: The religious and philosophical tradition founded by Siddhartha Gautama, centered on the Four Noble Truths, the Eightfold Path, and liberation from suffering.
The diverse family of Indian religious traditions rooted in the Vedas and Upanishads, encompassing ritual, philosophy, and diverse theologies.
keys.ambiguity_notes: 'Hinduism' as a unified category is somewhat anachronistic — prefer more specific labels where applicable.

keys.description: The direct or indirect effect of one thinker, text, or tradition on the development of another.
The method of philosophical inquiry through question-and-answer, associated with Socrates; aimed at exposing inconsistency and reaching clearer understanding.
The philosophical position that the true reality consists of abstract, eternal Forms of which the material world is a shadow.
The system of formal inference developed by Aristotle in the Organon, particularly the theory of the syllogism; the dominant framework of Western logic until modern times.
The philosophical tradition deriving from Aristotle's Lyceum, encompassing logic, natural science, ethics, and politics.
Karl Jaspers's concept of the roughly simultaneous emergence of philosophical and religious breakthroughs across Greece, India, China, and the Near East, c. 800–200 BCE.
keys.ambiguity_notes: The concept is analytical and retrospective, not recognized by the people living through it.

keys.description: The spread of cultural practices, ideas, and technologies from one society to another through contact.
The processes by which empires weaken and fragment — military overextension, fiscal crisis, succession conflicts, external pressure.
The hierarchical arrangement of society into groups with differential access to resources, power, and prestige.
The institution of human beings as property; a foundational economic and social institution of many ancient societies.
The ways in which communities preserve, transmit, and reconstruct their pasts through ritual, narrative, monument, and practice.
The claim to speak for or interpret the divine will; oracular traditions, prophetic movements, and revelatory texts.
The conduct of relations between polities through negotiation, treaty, and formal representation.
The physical geographic and environmental conditions that shape historical development — rivers, mountains, climate, resources.
The deliberate establishment of settlements in new territories, extending the political, cultural, or economic reach of a parent society.
The record of an individual's life, including birth, education, career, relationships, and death.
keys.ambiguity_notes: Used primarily as an edge key for biographical connections between persons.

keys.description: The direct pedagogical relationship between a teacher and student as a primary vector for intellectual transmission.
The practice of producing systematic commentaries on canonical texts as a mode of intellectual inquiry and transmission.
The founding and maintenance of institutional structures — academies, libraries, courts, religious orders — that sustain intellectual and cultural life.
Large-scale movement of peoples across geographic space, driven by conquest, famine, climate, or opportunity.
